data.title: KinderCare Learning Companies: Aided By Pandemic Subsidies
data.description:
Shares of KinderCare Learning Companies, Inc. (NYSE:KLC) have seen a solid welcome as a publicly traded company after it went public in recent times.

The child care provider trades at a demanding earnings multiple if we back out the pandemic-related subsidies still being received, making me cautious to get involved here, despite the secular growth displayed by the business.

Confidence For Life

The paragraph header is the mission of KinderCare Learning Companies, as the company understands the lifeline which child care provides to young families which aim to balance work with personal lives.

The company was founded back in 1969 and aims to help hard-working families to pursue their dreams. From just one location at the time, the company has grown to operate over 2,000 locations at this point, all over the country across some 40 states.

The company employs some 43,000 workers who provide care, educate and empower kids with great child care, thereby aiding these young families. These kids range from as young as six weeks old, to 12 years of age, with these facilities having the capacity to educate over 200,000 children in total, on a full-time or part-time basis.

Within its portfolio the company runs and operates a few different brands such as KCLC, Creme School and Champions, each focusing on different age groups and areas of child care. The company was acquired by Partners Group in 2015 which grew the business organically, and inorganically, only to bring the company public nearly a decade later. This makes that this is not a quick turnaround private equity story, certainly as Partners continues to hold over 70% of the shares post the public offering.

The marketplace for the so-called US ECE market is huge, with nearly 27 million workers relying on child care every day, with total child care expenditures seen at $76 billion per annum. With recognition of this industry on the rise, as well as subsidy programs, the need for such services continued to be great, certainly in a tight labor market.

Valuation & IPO Thoughts

KinderCare aimed to sell 24 million shares in a preliminary price range set between $23 and $27 per share, with final pricing set just below the midpoint of the range at $24 per share. This made that gross proceeds were seen around $576 million, with no shares sold by existing shareholders.

With a total share count of just over 114 million shares, the company was valued at $2.74 billion at the offer price, or about $3.7 billion if we factor in a pro forma net debt load of around $950 million.

In the calendar year 2023, the company generated revenues of $2.51 billion, up 16% from 2022, with about a third of revenues generated from government (agencies) subsidies. Because of the labor-intensive nature of the business, operating income is relatively modest at $275 million, for low double-digit margins.

Most of these earnings went into servicing the debt. However, with pro forma net debt seen below a billion, a 5% cost of debt could result in pre-tax profits of $225 million, which after a 25% tax rate would allow for earnings around $1.40 per share. Leverage would still be elevated based on adjusted EBITDA of $266 million in 2023, for a 3.6 times leverage ratio.

These revenues are generated from a total of just over 2,500 centers, of which some 60% relate to early childhood education centers, and the remaining from before- and after-school sites (which on average generate far lower revenues, because of fewer hours attended too).

The company has seen solid growth in the first half of 2024, with sales seen up 6% to $1.34 billion in the six-month period. Operating earnings of $114 million translate into margins of 8%, down from double-digit percentages in the year before, amidst continued (labor) inflation costs and less COVID-19 stimulus funds. These still totaled $50 million in the first half of 2024!

After earnings came in at $1.40 per share in 2023, these likely come in meaningfully lower in 2024 amidst generic margin pressure and fewer COVID-19 subsidies being received. Given this obvious trend, I am somewhat surprised to see shares trade up to $29 per share, also considering that leverage is still on the higher side.

Concluding Thoughts

The truth is that the business is still feeling the impact of the pandemic, and actually in a good way. In 2023, the company still received $150 million in COVID-19 related stimulus payments, more than half the reported operating income of $275 million. Frankly, if we subtract these subsidies, the resulting operating profits of $125 million are very modest, certainly if we factor in interest expenses due on a near billion net debt load.

In fact, with interest expenses leaving just $75 million in pre-tax earnings, I see earnings at just around half a dollar per share on an after-tax basis. This translates into very demanding earnings multiples, amidst elevated leverage employed.

Other than the big valuation risk, in the sense that recent earnings are seeing an uplift from the COVID-19 subsidies, there are general risks. These include such as reversal of generally solid economic conditions, a reliance on subsidies, the impact of pandemic, geographic trends, competition and seasonality, among others.

Amidst all this, I am very cautious here, as I am erring on the cautious side given the higher earnings multiple if we normalize earnings (that is, excluding pandemic-related subsidies). Given all this, I am happy to see the events unfold from here, but see no reason to get involved just yet, as I do not care enough for the business.

data.title: Got Diversification? Access The 20 Biggest U.S. Stocks
data.description:
By Daniel Prince, CFA

Why Is Diversification An Important Investment Strategy?

It's been a good time to own stocks. Major indexes like the Dow Jones Industrial Average and S&P 500 have produced a string of record closes this year, and a broad measure of U.S. equities has more than doubled in the past five years.2 But picking stocks isn’t easy and the harsh reality is nearly 38% of publicly traded companies in the S&P U.S. Total Market Index are down in the past five years, with an average decline of 53.8%.

Don’t be a loser in a winning market

5-year returns as of 8/31/2024

So equity investors have done great but only if they were in the right stocks. In other words, it’s been very possible to experience meaningful losses in a winning market, which shows the importance of diversification in investing.

And while past performance doesn’t guarantee future returns, shares of the 20 largest publicly traded U.S. companies contributed 60% of the S&P 500’s total return over the last five-years.3 The iShares Top 20 U.S. Stocks ETF (NYSEARCA:TOPT), which launched on Oct. 24, 2024, empowers investors to access 20 of the biggest and some of the most successful companies in the U.S. with a single trade, via the convenience of the low-cost and tax-efficient ETF wrapper.

The iShares Top 20 U.S. Stocks ETF seeks to track the investment results of an index comprised of the 20 largest U.S. companies by market capitalization within the S&P 500 Index. Market cap is the dollar value of a company’s total outstanding shares of public stock. If, for example, a company has 100 million shares of stock outstanding and it trades at $100 per share, its market cap would be $1 trillion.

Market capitalization is often used as a way to measure the size of a company, and as a way to differentiate between companies. For instance, companies are typically categorized as being large-, mid- or small-cap based on their market capitalization.

Brands currently in the top 20 by market cap include Apple, Amazon, Meta, Johnson & Johnson and JPMorgan Chase. The 20 largest publicly traded U.S. stocks collectively represent $22.6 trillion in market cap, almost equal to the size of the U.S. economy, which produced approximately $25 trillion of GDP in the first quarter of 2024.4

These companies operate in more than 160 countries around the world and currently employ about 4.4 million Americans.5

Meet the companies in the iShares Top 20 U.S. Stocks ETF*

Caption:Table showcasing several facts about the top 20 largest publicly traded U.S. stocks, including: market cap, sector, major products, and index weighting in the iShares Top 20 U.S. Stocks ETF (TOPT) as of 9/23/2024.

Name (ticker) Market cap (bln) Sector Major product(s) Year of top 20 inclusion Index weight

Microsoft Corp. (MSFT) $3,235 Information Technology Azure, Microsoft 365, Windows, LinkedIn, Xbox 1994 14.89%

Costco Wholesale (COST) $402 Consumer Staples Retail membership 2023 2.27%
Johnson & Johnson (JNJ) $395 Health Care Pharmaceuticals, medical technologies 2024 *** 2.23%
Home Depot Inc. (HD) $387 Consumer Discretionary Home improvement retail 1999 2.18%
AbbVie Inc. (ABBV) $342 Health Care Biopharmaceuticals 2024 **** 1.93%

* Source: S&P Global Indices as of 9/23/24. Subject to change.

** Eli Lilly in the top 20 in 1997 but dropped out until returning in 2022.

*** JNJ has been in and out of the top 20 since 1995, including from 2004–2020.

**** AbbVie was in the Top 20 in 2022, but not 2023.

Sectors based on the Global Industry Classification Standard (GICS), which consists of 11 sectors: Energy, Materials, Industrials, Consumer Discretionary, Consumer Staples, Health Care, Financials, Information Technology, Communication Services, Utilities, Real Estate.

20 Big Stocks, One Easy Trade

Here are three potential benefits of the iShares Top 20 U.S. Stocks ETF:

  • Access: TOPT is designed to track the performance of the 20 largest U.S. stocks by market capitalization. You may be new to investing, but you’re probably familiar with these brands, which are some of the largest companies in the technology, consumer goods, communications, healthcare and financial services sectors.
  • Diversification: As noted above, a rising equity tide hasn’t lifted all boats and TOPT provides the benefits of diversification vs single-stock ownership. In theory, any potential weakness in one or two of the top 20 may be partially offset by strength in other constituents.
  • Convenience: Just like an appetizer sampler removes the challenge of having to choose a favorite starter, TOPT is a one-ticker solution that gives an investor exposure to a variety of large-cap U.S. stocks with no minimum investment other than the price of the ETF itself. With TOPT, investors can get exposure to the 20 largest U.S. stocks at a fraction of the capital required to purchase them individually; it could cost nearly $7,000 to purchase a single share of each of the top 20 stocks included in the fund.6

As with all equity index ETFs, TOPT is designed to reflect the price of a collection of stocks - in this case, the 20-largest U.S. companies by market capitalization.

Just like the Billboard Top 20 is regularly updated - based on the sales, airplay and streaming of songs - TOPT adjusts as the market changes. TOPT is professionally managed against an index that dynamically rebalances on a quarterly basis - the third Friday of March, June, September, and December - ensuring investors maintain their exposure to the 20 largest companies on a rolling basis, though it may not reflect current rankings between rebalances.

With the rebalancing done for you automatically, TOPT takes the guesswork out of keeping up to date with the latest corporate trends.

data.title: Carlyle Group: Taking Profit On Another Sharp Rally (Rating Downgrade)
data.description:
The recent performance of Carlyle Group (NASDAQ:CG) has once again surpassed our expectations, delivering an impressive 32.3% gain in just six weeks since we aggressively upgraded our rating from a "Sell" to a "Strong Buy" in September.

Our bullish view was predominantly driven by the deep discount on Carlyle's shares relative to its peers, including Blackstone (BX), Apollo (APO), and KKR (KKR). However, from a broader perspective, there was also compelling evidence of a rapidly improving outlook for AUM growth across private markets.

Recent news from the alternative asset industry has reinforced our bullish view on private market AUM growth and fee-related earnings. Apollo announced plans this month to grow fee-related earnings at an average annual growth of 20% in the next five years. Blackstone also reported better-than-expected results this month, with the firm deploying capital at the fastest pace in two years.

Signs Of Greed Present Tactical Opportunity To Recycle Capital

From a tactical perspective, however, we see a compelling opportunity to further enhance overall portfolio performance by realizing gains on Carlyle and recycling capital for opportunities that are likely to deliver superior risk-adjusted returns.

We recently published an article in which we also realized an annualized gain of around 37% on our bullish position on the SPDR S&P Regional Banking ETF (KRE). In that article, we discussed why time is a major factor in determining whether tactical moves to recycle capital make sense and why allocating capital to the most productive asset and maximizing returns-to-risk within any given investment horizon is critical.

For example, achieving a 20%-30% return on a stock should be considered a decent outcome for most investors during any given year. But if that return is met or exceeded well before the end of the original investment horizon, then investors are better off taking profit on a big winning position and redeploying capital elsewhere. Even redeploying capital towards short-dated bonds or money-market funds for the remaining investment horizon would incrementally add to returns while dramatically reducing risk for the portfolio.

In the case of our bullish position on Carlyle, we have already achieved a 32.3% gain in just six weeks. That kind of return would typically require several months or quarters to generate, assuming the same level of risk. Although we still like the long-term fundamentals for alternative asset managers, we should balance that against the downside risks of holding on to an unusually profitable and potentially highly volatile position.

Separating Value From Froth

There are several reasons why unusually profitable trades may potentially turn highly volatile.

Firstly, for a trade to achieve an abnormally large return within an extremely short period of time, it would typically require either a fresh catalyst (positive and material news) or some form of market imbalance (sudden surge in demand for a company's shares). So, if a big move was driven by positive and material news, then those abnormally large returns are more likely to be sustained. In fact, prices can move even higher as more buyers become attracted to the stock and attempt to bid up the price or as sellers demand higher prices for their shares.

But when a big move appears to be driven by an unexplained surge in demand for the stock, then those abnormally large returns might be driven by speculative trading or a lack of market liquidity in the short term. Because speculative trading can be fickle and a lack of market liquidity works both ways, the stock may potentially suffer equally large losses when sentiment turns.

Of course, in Carlyle's case, one possible reason could be bargain hunters scooping up its heavily discounted shares. But this also means that these buyers, just like us, may be thinking of realizing those abnormally large gains soon.

Secondly, unusually profitable trades may be caused by market mispricing. Many veteran traders would agree that in the short term, stock prices regularly overshoot on the upside as well as the downside. Mean reversion trading strategies take advantage of this mispricing by buying when prices overshoot to the downside and selling when prices overshoot to the upside, especially when such moves are unexplained or speculative in nature.

Therefore, unusually profitable trades could be a sign of mispricing. And when the mispricing is severe, competent traders would jump at the opportunity. That, of course, would mean prices could reverse towards a more reasonable range very quickly.

The tricky part is figuring out exactly how much of a large price movement is justified by improving fundamentals and how much of it is pure speculation. We believe most traders are simply incapable of precisely separating value from froth. We certainly are not capable of that. However, precision is not necessary in such cases. Whenever prices move well beyond our expectations, we use that as an indicator or a warning sign to at least consider taking some profit off the table.

Taking profit on our Carlyle position and redeploying the proceeds elsewhere not only provides the potential to stretch returns through other ideas, but also effectively reduces risk, especially if we roll those returns into less risky assets.

Rising Bond Yields & Extremely Greedy Sentiment On Equities

Since we are recommending investors to redeploy gains from Carlyle, we should perhaps share some ideas on exactly where to put that money to work.

One good option is to redeploy those gains to fixed income. Bond yields have rebounded lately due to market expectations of smaller rate cuts by the Federal Reserve and potentially larger fiscal deficits after the U.S. presidential election. As the accompanying chart shows, yields on two-year U.S. treasuries are currently trading at around 4.06%.

This is good news for fixed-income investors, given our view that widespread defaults are unlikely given the robust macroeconomic outlook and that we continue to see evidence of disinflation.

The SPDR® Bloomberg 1-3 Month T-Bill ETF (BIL) may be a good option for investors that prefer even better liquidity. BIL is designed to track the performance of U.S. Treasury Bills that have remaining maturities of between 1 and 3 months.

According to information provided by the fund issuer State Street Global Advisors, BIL seeks to provide investment results that, before fees and expenses, correspond generally to the price and yield performance of the Bloomberg 1-3 Month U.S. Treasury Bill Index. This Index includes all publicly issued U.S. Treasury Bills with remaining maturity of between 1 and 3 months, are rated investment-grade and denominated in U.S. dollars, and must have a fixed rate.

According to fund data as of 21 October 2024, BIL has a yield to maturity of 4.73% and an average maturity of 0.10 years (1.2 months).

For aggressive investors, returns from fixed income may seem uninspiring compared to equities. Therefore, it is understandable that ideas yielding 4.0%—4.7% may seem uninspiring to some. However, even aggressive investors should understand that equities provide better returns because they also come with greater risks. These risks also tend to peak when market sentiment on equities is overwhelmingly bullish or at "Extreme Greed".

Just two days ago, we published an article highlighting that market sentiment on the S&P 500 Index (SPX) has reached "Extreme Greed," and we downgraded our rating to "Hold." Our broader equity market view on the SPX closely aligns with our recent recommendations to take profit on KRE and now Carlyle.

In the event of a broader equity market correction, we expect the big winners to give up a significant share of their recent gains. By tactically rotating portfolio exposure away from these big winners and into value or less risky assets, we are positioning ourselves to be aggressive on risk again should prices pull back. More importantly, we reduce overall portfolio risk while locking in returns from our biggest winners.

To be clear, we maintain our bullish long-term view of Carlyle and alternative asset managers in general, but we are making a tactical decision to take profit on Carlyle with the view of re-entering at a more favourable price.

Risk To Outlook

Of course, we could be completely wrong about Carlyle. Especially given that Carlyle is still currently trading at an undemanding forward P/E multiple of 13.8x and that we see a favourable macroeconomic environment for alternative asset managers, there is certainly potential for the stock to climb further in the coming months.

There is also the possibility that Carlyle's valuation had been so deeply discounted before that even after a spectacular rally, there is still room for further gains.

But we prefer taking good positions instead of making perfect trades.

In Conclusion

Accordingly, we are taking profit on our bullish view on Carlyle and downgrading our rating from "Strong Buy" to "Sell". However, should we see a decent pullback of around 10% in the coming weeks, we would consider re-establishing our long-term bullish view on Carlyle.

data.title: Old Dominion: A Classy Operator But Not Quite Cheap Enough
data.description:
In a commodity business, the one thing that can set you apart is operational excellence. Some companies have brand value, some companies have a patent, others have a differentiated product, but in a commodity market, where the product is broadly uniform, prices are more-or-less dictated to the providers, meaning that their operational efficiency is the only difference between success and failure.

The trucking business is not quite a commodity business - there are better and worse ways to get things from A to B - but many sources of competitive advantage which apply in other industries do not apply here. Old Dominion Freight Line (NASDAQ:ODFL) is a classy operator in this competitive business. The stock price appreciation over the past 10-20 years shows that: its 10-year total return is 750% compared to the S&P 500’s 190%. But recently the stock has underperformed, roughly flat over a 1-year time horizon while the S&P has risen ~38%.

Though this recent underperformance looks like it might present an opportunity, especially as we could be due for an upswing for the trucking industry, I think the stock is currently trading around fair value, leaving no margin of safety if my projections for the business’s performance over the long-term turn-out to be wrong. Therefore, though I admire Old Dominion’s business model and consistent execution, and I do own shares, I can only recommend a hold at these prices.

The Company

Operations

Old Dominion Freight Line provides less-than-truckload (LTL) motor carriage services. Put simply, this means that they provide freight by road for businesses that need to send shipments which are too large for FedEx or UPS (>150 lbs) but not large enough to fill a whole truck (<15,000 lbs).

Old Dominion operates a network of regional hubs and local “spokes” called Pickup & Delivery (P&D) Centers. The P&D Centers, as the name suggests, deal with the pickup and delivery of the shipments from/to businesses or consumers in the local area. The regional hubs organize shipments by destination and move them between regions and across the country. As of June 2024, it operated 261 service centers in the 48 contiguous states.

Customers either pay a tariff determined by the company’s internal freight-costing system or, for larger customers, agree a negotiated service contract.

Differentiation

In an industry like logistics, it is difficult for a company to set itself apart. The job is to move a thing from point A to point B safely, within a predetermined time period and at a reasonable cost. Many companies can do that. There are pockets where specialism is required - say moving very high-value items - but for the most part, the job is simple enough that many companies can offer a similar service. It’s no wonder then that the market is competitive and fragmented.

The way for a company to set itself apart in this industry is to display operational excellence, and over the past decade and more, Old Dominion has been doing just that. As I mentioned, one important KPI for this industry is on-time delivery (OTD). This chart from ODFL’s investor presentation shows that it has improved markedly on that measure in the last 20 years to the point where it almost always delivers on time.

It’s difficult to find data from other carriers, and estimates of the industry average for on-time delivery range between 85% and 97% from the various sources I have looked at. But regardless, we can say that ODFL is operating above the industry average. What I find particularly impressive is the very small dip in 2021 during the supply chain disruptions caused by the pandemic.

Another KPI is the loss ratio, i.e. how many shipments are damaged in transit. ODFL measures this by cargo claims as a percentage of revenue, and it has also been going in the right direction:

A 0.1% loss ratio is as close to perfect as you are likely to get.

But we want outside confirmation that Old Dominion is a classy outfit. The company was recently named the number 1 national LTL carrier for quality by Mastio & Company. But the impressive thing is that they have been accorded this title for the 15th year in a row! This survey was based on interviews with 1,600 logistics professionals across the US, so is probably a more reliable gauge of how popular the service is than an industry award, which is often judged by small groups of “experts”, and may thus be affected by industry politics. The press release states:

‘Shippers and logistics professionals consistently responded with OD as their top carrier, ranking OD #1 in an impressive 23 of the 28 evaluated categories, including “carrier is trustworthy,” “shipments delivered when promised,” and “ease of doing business with the carrier.”’

This chart from ODFL, based on data from Mastio's 2023 survey, shows that Old Dominion offers by far the best service. While they charge premium prices, they are by no means the most expensive, and Mastio believes they are charging fairly.

Further confirmation of ODFL's superior offering comes from net promoter scores (NPS) and customer satisfaction scores (CSAT). Here is a comparison of Old Dominion and some of its competitors:

Data from comparably.com

Old Dominion comes out on top on both metrics, showing they have a clear lead when it comes to customer satisfaction.

Financials

This lead should show up in market share growth and superior operating metrics to competitors. ODFL has demonstrated both, growing market share from around 2.9% of the LTL market in 2002 to 12% today. Its margins, return on invested capital and lack of debt compare favorably to its competitors:

6.7%

1.1

Old Dominion has the highest FCF margin over a 10-year period, the second-highest return on invested capital and the lowest debt.

A note: all of these are public companies that provide LTL services. Some of them do not have LTL as their main line of business, so there is no direct comparison. There are also some large private companies in the LTL space, but it’s obviously not possible to compare their financials.

The Culture

I think part of Old Dominion’s operating superiority over its competitors comes from its culture. Until recently, it was a family-run business - the CEO was the grandson of the founders. This report from McKinsey explored the reasons for the outperformance of family businesses over time and concluded that there were four main reasons:

  1. They focus on purpose beyond profits

  2. They take a long-term perspective and reinvest in the business

  3. They are financially conservative and cautious about debt and high-risk investments

  4. Their internal processes allow for efficient decision-making

I think the history of Old Dominion displays aspects of all these.

Regarding the first, the company has been relentlessly focused in the last 20 years on improving the quality of their service by increasing on-time deliveries and reducing loss rates. This has been achieved partly through an extensive Driver Training Program. They made a significant investment in creating and running this training program and the result has been better safety, lower loss rates, higher on-time deliveries and improved driver retention (the 10-year average turnover rate for driver graduates is 7.4%).

This example also covers the second point about taking a long-term perspective and reinvesting in the business. Another example is the fact that during cyclical downturns in the trucking industry, they have historically not laid off drivers. This is the move that most companies would make to protect profits in the next year or two, but Old Dominion shows loyalty to its drivers, which is reciprocated when the cycle turns up.

One of the reasons they are able to do this is linked to point three. The company is certainly financially conservative with close to zero debt. They have not made any large acquisitions in the past 10 years, despite having opportunities to do so, e.g. with the recent bankruptcy of a large competitor.

The fourth point is a little harder to determine, but there are examples of the company making big decisions which have improved its operations. One was the standardization of practices across all Service Centers. Though individual Service Centers were operating quite efficiently with their different methods, the management realized that employee retention and overall efficiency would be improved if employees could move round the country and immediately be equally productive in any Service Center. Thus, they initiated a process of standardization which was difficult but set the company up for greater success.

The company was run by members of the Congdon family from its foundation in 1934 until 2018. Earl Congdon Jr and David Congdon, both former CEOs, still sit on the board. The current CEO is Kevin “Marty” Freeman. He took over in 2023 and has been with the company since 1992. It doesn’t surprise me at all that the company has promoted from within. It’s too early to judge whether he will adequately continue the legacy of the Congdon family, but he surely has a good understanding of the value of the company and the reasons for its success.

Another positive of family-owned businesses is that they generally have good insider alignment. Insiders own about 10% of the company. As of the latest proxy, Kevin Freeman owns 64,432 shares, with a market value of around $12.8m. Given he has worked at Old Dominion since he was 19, I presume that this accounts for the vast majority of his net worth. The executive compensation plan states that the base salary is only 8% of the total potential annual compensation. The rest, a mixture of cash and stock awards, is based on performance. Furthermore, the company has a stock ownership policy, which sets a minimum amount of stock executives must hold as a multiple of their annual base salary. For example, the CEO must hold 6x his base salary in ODFL shares.

I like to see this institutionalized insider alignment. It’s one of the many things that demonstrate that the company knows where its strength comes from.

The Future

Old Dominion’s annualized revenue growth over the past decade is around 10%. I think it should be able to sustain this. The LTL trucking market has grown at a 4.5% CAGR for the last 20 years, and Old Dominion’s market share has gone from 2.9% to 12%. I see no reason why the company can’t continue to take market share over the next 10 years, given the superior service it provides, its robust operating model and culture and its financial position. If the industry continues to grow at a 4.5% CAGR over the next 10 years, which, I think, is achievable merely with a combination of real GDP growth and inflation, and Old Dominion increases LTL market share from 12% to 20%, it can achieve 10% revenue growth. Given its market share roughly doubled in each of the last 10-year periods, I think an increase of two thirds over the next 10 years is well within the range of possibility.

What about earnings? The business has experienced significant operating leverage over the past 10 years, with operating margin and net margin nearly doubling. I doubt that this pace of improvement in profitability can be maintained. However, there are some economies of scale in this business, and if the company can grow revenue, margins should continue to improve if at a slower rate.

The company is continually producing plenty of free cash flow, which means it could use it to reduce share count. They have been doing this - the share count is down each of the last 10 years - but the pace of buybacks has not been dizzying, about 1.5% a year. I expect this to increase, though. As the company throws off more and more cash, it cannot all be used for CapEx, so more of it will be allocated to shareholder return. With small margin improvements and buybacks, I think it will be possible to attain 15% EPS growth over the next 10 years.

The Risks

Cyclicality

Trucking is very much a cyclical industry, which tends to move up and down with economic (especially industrial) activity. We can see this in ODFL’s revenue growth, which averages 10% over the last 10 years but has had consecutive years of 6.6% and 0.6% (2015/16) and 30.9% and 19.1% (2021/22). However, unlike many cyclical stocks, ODFL has managed to consistently maintain profitability in downturns. It has been a public company since 1991, and it has made a profit every year.

Although it’s certainly something to be aware of, I don’t think cyclicality poses existential risks for the company. Investors just need to be prepared for some draw-downs over a long holding period. On the bright side, these could be good opportunities to buy.

The trucking industry has been in a cyclical downturn for a few years now, as indicated by the Truck Tonnage Index (for volume) and the PPI for trucking (for pricing):

Tonnage has not recovered from its 2019 peak and prices spiked during the pandemic and are normalizing from the peak in 2022. I can’t tell how long this will go on for, but I can say that it’s generally a better idea to buy cyclicals during a down cycle than at the top.

Note: this data is for trucking as a whole. While there is no FRED data specifically for the Less-than-Truckload sub-industry, it accords with the pattern seen by ODFL.

Loss of Competitive Advantage

The competitive advantages I have listed in my analysis of the company are real and do constitute a moat in my opinion. However, the moat is a fairly narrow one because the advantages ODFL has need to be actively maintained. This “Process Power”, as identified by Hamilton Helmer, consists in the experience, expertise and mindset of the management. If they start to stray from the current values of the business or try new ideas that don’t work, this Process Power could evaporate pretty quickly.

For example, if they choose to take on debt and make unwise acquisitions, the financial position could deteriorate. If they start cutting corners and decide to provide a cheaper service to gain market share that ends up being less reliable, margins (and maybe ultimately growth) could suffer. If they allow the working conditions and remuneration for their employees to deteriorate, it could lead to unionization, which would result in much less flexibility.

None of these mistakes seem to be on the horizon, but I just mention them to point out that a moat based on process power is narrower than say that of Coca-Cola (KO) which has a priceless brand or Mastercard (MA) and Visa (V), which benefit from incredibly powerful network effects. Warren Buffett has said that he likes to own a business so good that an idiot could run it. I’m not sure that description applies to Old Dominion, but fortunately the management team appears to know what they are doing.

Automation

One potential risk that seems quite distant but is still worth considering is that of automation and autonomous vehicles. Let me paint the scenario. Tesla is about to start ramping up production of its Semi truck. Potential benefits of the product include:

  • Improved performance in acceleration and hill climbs owing to instant torque

  • Lower energy costs

  • Lower cost of vehicles

But beyond this, if Tesla is able to improve their self-driving software so that the trucks can operate autonomously, those who quickly adopt this new technology will have an advantage over those who don’t. This is because Tesla will price the software below the wage of a human driver in order to encourage adoption.

If Old Dominion were to get caught on the wrong side of this equation, it could make its services uncompetitive, leading to a loss of market share and forced price cuts which would lower margins.

However, thinking through the likely scenarios, I imagine that Old Dominion would most likely get on the right side of the new paradigm for the following reasons:

  • Their workers are not unionized. Truck companies whose workers are unionized will feel their wrath as soon as they start trying to automate.

  • Automation would not replace all their drivers. It would only be useful for “line-haul” routes - those between hubs in the system - while P&D routes would still need a driver to load and unload items. This might help with the labor relations aspect.

  • They are in a strong financial position which would allow them to spend a lot on CapEx in a short period of time. Other, more levered companies might put their solvency at risk by going all in on new vehicles, charging infrastructure, etc.

  • They are a large customer. If there is a rush for orders of this product, larger customers are likely to be prioritized. Although some truckload (TL) companies may be ahead of Old Dominion, they will likely be near the front of the queue.

Even if Old Dominion were to get out ahead of this change, I can still see the disruption being painful, with higher CapEx requirements weighing on free cash flow, asset value write-offs if they have to retire diesel trucks early, low morale and perhaps discontent among the workforce.

As I say, this seems at the moment like a remote possibility, but given that Waymo is already operating robotaxis in certain geo-fenced areas, so it's conceivable in the near(ish) future that autonomous trucks could drive common trucking highway routes between hubs. In any case, we are trying to think of risks the company will face over the long term, so the scenario is worth considering.

Valuation

ODFL is currently trading at around 35x forward earnings, which is expensive compared to its history. To mitigate this, we can say that a) it should have a premium multiple given its superiority to its peer group and b) since the trucking industry is in a down cycle, the earnings may be at a cyclical low and ready for an upswing, so the PE ratio is not the best measure of valuation. However, it’s also trading at elevated levels on Price/Book and pretty much any metric you could choose. Seeking Alpha’s valuation grade of D- confirms that the stock is, on its face, expensive:

Furthermore, I looked back at the PE coming out of previous cyclical downturns, and it was around 15x. Because Old Dominion is quite good at maintaining good earnings even during downturns, the phenomenon that often occurs in cyclical stocks where they have a high PE at the bottom doesn’t seem to happen here.

I created a model to estimate the returns that investors could see for ODFL over the next 10 years. I made the following assumptions:

  • Revenue: 10% annual growth for the reasons given above.

  • Net Margin: slight margin expansion on account of increasing scale.

  • Shares outstanding: reducing around 3% a year - picking up the pace compared to recent history. Judging by their response to a question on the most recent earnings call, management will only buy back stock aggressively at lower multiples. I have assumed lower multiples going forward and therefore accelerated buybacks. If the multiple does not deflate, shares outstanding won’t decline as quickly, but the net effect on share price should be close to neutral.

  • P/E: multiple comes down closer to historical levels.

These are the results:

As you can see, the model suggests that the market is valuing ODFL quite fairly if my assumptions are in the right ballpark. Adding the small dividend yield of about 0.5%, shareholders would see a return slightly above the historical market average of 10%. I do expect the dividend yield to increase over time as the dividend has grown much faster than earnings since it was initiated in 2017 and the payout ratio is still very low. Even so, I wouldn’t expect the average dividend yield over the period to be much above 1%.

For most companies, I would look for a substantial margin of safety to recommend a buy, ideally 50%. However, my qualitative analysis of Old Dominion, combined with the fact that it appears the trucking industry is some way through a slow period, means that I would be comfortable buying with a margin of safety of more like 25%. With regard to the model above, that means a buy price of around $150, which is why I rate the shares a hold.

data.title: VWO: Buy The Post-China-Stimulus Dip
data.description:
Emerging markets, as measured by the Vanguard FTSE Emerging Markets Index Fund ETF Shares (NYSEARCA:VWO), are having their best 12-month performance relative to ex-US developed markets since early 2021. The absolute and relative rally comes following a hefty dose of stimulus from authorities in China over the past month-plus.

Questions remain, however, regarding how targeted the liquidity injection will be. China has a history of offering modest stimulus, which doesn’t seem to help its consumers very much. Will it be different this time? Will that result in a new bull market in what has been a very weak area of the global stock market? Will such a move result in broader EM strength?

These are tough questions with still-hazy answers. Nevertheless, I am upgrading my rating on VWO from a hold to a buy. I was neutral on the fund late last year. Concerning me was a soft technical setup despite EM's generally cheap valuation. Weak data out of China and global growth fears away from the developed markets arena seemed considerable.

Since then, though, the ETF has returned a strong 20.7%, though that has been an underperformance to the S&P 500 which is up 26.2% in that span. Let’s go through VWO’s fundamentals and technicals as the dust settles in China following a strong September and early October advance.

EM Outperforming ex-US DM by 4ppt YoY

EM Equities Near 13x Earnings

According to the issuer, VWO invests in shares of companies located in emerging markets around the world, such as China, Brazil, Taiwan, and South Africa. Its goal is to closely track the return of the FTSE Emerging Markets All Cap China A Inclusion Index, according to Vanguard.

VWO is a large ETF with more than $115 billion in assets under management as of October 23, 2024. That is up materially from just $98 billion in December 2023. The ETF sports a low 0.08% annual expense ratio while its forward dividend yield is high at 2.55% - more than a percentage point above the S&P 500’s yield. Holding the fund in a taxable brokerage account has the potential to offer holders a foreign tax credit, so that’s something to consider from an asset location perspective.

Share-price momentum has improved markedly in the past few months, evidenced by its robust B+ ETF Grade by Seeking Alpha’s quantitative scoring system. Its risk rating at a B- might surprise readers as EM stocks have actually not been all that volatile this year despite the recent swings in the Chinese equity market. Finally, liquidity is healthy given VWO’s high average daily volume of more than 9 million shares and a median 30-day bid/ask spread of just two basis points, per Vanguard.

Digging into the portfolio, the 3-star, Bronze-rated ETF by Morningstar plots along the top row of the style box, indicating its high weighting in the large-cap space. There is, however, a material 22% exposure to non-US SMID caps. Also from a diversification point of view, there is a decent mix of value and growth stocks.

With a price-to-earnings ratio under 14 and relative strength perking up, the valuation and momentum situations could be coming together. Considering that VWO’s long-term earnings growth rate is firm at 12.3%, the resulting PEG ratio is barely above 1, indicating a strong value.

VWO: Portfolio & Factor Profiles

VWO has diversification at the sector level too. Financials and the Information Technology sector are each above one-fifth of the fund. That’s a cyclical-value sector and a growth sector. Consumer Discretionary has been relatively weak in 2024, and it’s the third-largest holding in VWO.

Materials and Energy are overweights versus their makeup in the SPX, but resource areas are much smaller pieces of EM today compared to, say, the mid-2000s.

VWO: Holdings & Dividend Information

Seasonally, there are some bullish tailwinds today. The late October through January period has been among the better calendar stretches for VWO in the last decade. Keep in mind that overall performance has been rather poor since 2014 for nearly all EM funds.

VWO: Bullish October-January Trends

The Technical Take

VWO shareholders have enjoyed strong performance since October 2023. The fund returned more than 36% from the October 2023 low to the most recent peak above $49. Notice in the chart below that shares reached technical overbought conditions in late September following the China bazooka stimulus announcement. That resulted in a price gap near the $46 level that may get filled in the coming days which could offer a favorable entry point.

Also take a look at the rising long-term 200-day moving average – the trend indicator line is below the rising 50dma with price above both. That’s a positive setup for momentum investors, particularly considering a long-term breakout that occurred earlier this year. If we take a measured move price forecast based on the $37 to $43 range from late 2022 through the first half of this year, then shares hit the target nearly precisely earlier this month. That makes the current pullback understandable.

Overall, with a key price gap at $46, we are nearing a critical support spot on VWO which offers an entry point heading into 2025.

VWO: Shares Pullback From Overbought Conditions, Near A Key Price Gap, Rising 200dma

The Bottom Line

I have a buy rating on VWO. I see the EM ETF as solid on valuation, with better momentum trends today.

data.title: Colony Bankcorp, Inc. 2024 Q3 - Results - Earnings Call Presentation
data.description: The following slide deck was published by Colony Bankcorp, Inc. in conjunction with their 2024 Q3 earnings call.

data.title: Home Sales Hit 14-Year Lows, Pain For Real Estate Continues
data.description:
Introduction

This week, home sales in the US hit a 14-year low, down to the lowest levels since 2010 in the aftermath of the Great Financial Crisis. This is largely, in my opinion, due to the fact that mortgage rates did not fall after the Fed cut the Fed Funds Rate last month. In fact, mortgage rates rose.

Because more Fed cuts seem likely, it's also likely that homebuyers are sitting out of the market and waiting for better rates, which they assume will come in the next few months. It's unclear whether that will come true or not, as the Fed could pivot if the macroeconomic situation changes, but for now, investors are waiting in anticipation, and it means that home sales are dwindling.

Current Home Sales Are Down

The numbers look a little stark because of the scale of this chart, but you can see from high to low, there's a drop of about half a million homes being sold that month.

Keep in mind that these are existing homes, not new homes.

We can see that since February '24, the velocity has been slowing, and there is a clear downtrend in sales month-over-month. So why is this?

Home Prices are Up

My first answer is that home prices aren't budging. They are still very, very high. Combine this with a higher mortgage rate that isn't following the Fed Funds Rate, and you've got a double-whammy that prices out many homebuyers. The same house from 2021 may cost nearly twice as much today for a new homebuyer because of the amortized payments.

Data from Redfin Corporation (RDFN) reinforces this, where homes on their platform have risen 3.9% in price YoY, and home sales have fallen 5% YoY.

Inventory is Down, Despite Existing Homes Up for Sale

Inventory is down, badly, especially existing homes. When we look at the monthly supply of housing, we're looking at a metric that tells us how many months we could expect to sell the amount of inventory currently on the market.

Since the beginning of the year, we've been trending up, and are now at 4.3, meaning that it would take 4.3 months to sell all the existing homes on the market, assuming no new homes are added. This may look high, until we take a look at its comparison to new homes.

Looking at this last chart, we can see that while existing homes are trending up, we are still in the same glut of new homes that are not selling, or are at least being replaced as fast as they sell.

It's odd for us to be in a market where there are so many more new homes for sale than existing homes. Why is this?

The Lock-In Effect

Because there was a time not so long ago where mortgage rates were very low, many homebuyers got mortgages at sub 5%, meaning that it doesn't make a ton of economic sense for them to sell their home and get a new mortgage at a higher rate. For the same amount of house, they will raise their payments.

Since 2008, we've seen the vast majority of homebuyers achieve <5% mortgages, which means that we should expect this kind of inventory pain whenever the mortgage rate is above this figure, as it will amplify the lock-in effect.

This effect will further constrain housing inventory so long as rates remain where they are.

We can see from history that the mortgage rate doesn't drop below 5% unless the Fed Funds Rate is close to 3% or less.

Falling to a sub-5% mortgage rate will require a lot of cuts from the Fed, and is unlikely to happen anytime soon. The Fed is projecting their rates to drop to 3% in 2026, where they can be kept for the longer run. That is an ideal scenario, of course, and not likely to pan out exactly as they predict.

What Does This Mean For Real Estate?

Well, it means that housing prices are likely to stay elevated for now, especially as rates hover far above 5%. Those rates will stay high so long as the central bank rate is above 3%, which looks to be a while away from now.

I don't believe that residential real estate has a shot at recovering sooner, with existing inventory still at a low, and new inventory unable to sell quickly as it comes with a very high price tag (thanks to those higher rates).

If you are looking to become a homebuyer, look for newer homes as they are more plentiful in the market, and are more likely to experience price cuts to move inventory. If you are looking to become a home seller, just be careful about the lock-in effect and consider how a newly originated mortgage might affect your payments.

Mortgage-backed securities, especially new issues, have been on a tear. I recommend looking at the ETF I think best tracks new issue MBS, the Simplify MBS ETF (MTBA). Simplify themselves wrote a Seeking Alpha piece on the fund earlier this year that I recommend checking out. Its yield is now above the index's and seems to be leveling off around 5%.

Conclusion

Ultimately, this new data coming out of residential housing is full of mixed signals. While it may be a good time to sell your home as prices are high, mortgage rates are higher despite the central bank rate coming down, and finding a new home may be difficult as the supply of existing homes is constrained due to the lock-in effect.

I caution investors who are looking into physical real estate to consider REITs and mortgage-backed securities at the current time, as they reduce risk and provide access to non-traditional funding options less affected by the 30-year mortgage rate.

Thanks for reading.

data.title: Money Supply Grows For 11 Straight Months
data.description:
Money Supply is a very important indicator. It helps show how tight or loose current monetary conditions are regardless of what the Fed is doing with interest rates. Even if the Fed is tight, if Money Supply is increasing, it has an inflationary effect.

One key metric shown below is the “Wenzel” 13-week annualized money supply figure. It was made popular by the late Robert Wenzel who tracked the metric weekly as an indicator for where the economy might be headed. In 2020, the Fed started reporting the data monthly instead of weekly. It should also be noted that Money Supply data can be heavily revised in future months.

Recent Trends

Seasonally Adjusted Money Supply is delayed by a month. The 11 consecutive periods of increased money supply are for Nov 2023 through September 2024. September growth was not as high as August, but was still another big month up.

Figure 1: MoM M2 Change (Seasonally Adjusted)

The September increase was 4.6% annualized.

Figure 2: M2 Growth Rates

That is slightly above the September average of +4.1%.

Figure 3: Average Monthly Growth Rates

Non-seasonally adjusted numbers show data through end of September, and show another big up month of $124B. This is inclusive of the entire month of September.

Figure 4: MoM M2 Change (Non-Seasonally Adjusted)

The weekly data below shows the spikes and dips.

Figure 5: WoW M2 Change

The “Wenzel” 13-week Money Supply

The late Robert Wenzel of Economic Policy Journal used a modified calculation to track Money Supply. He used a trailing 13-week average growth rate annualized as defined in his book The Fed Flunks. He specifically used the weekly data that was not seasonally adjusted. His analogy was that in order to know what to wear outside, he wants to know the current weather, not temperatures that have been averaged throughout the year.

The objective of the 13-week average is to smooth some of the choppy data without bringing in too much history that could blind someone from seeing what’s in front of them. The 13-week average growth rate can be seen in the table below.

Growth has been positive for 40 of the last 41 weeks, with only a very slight dip 11 weeks ago. Right now, the trend is definitely positive.

Figure 6: WoW Trailing 13-week Average Money Supply Growth

The plot below shows how this year compares with previous years. This year started a bit lower than previous years (though much higher than last year). It is now catching up to the previous years as we head into the Fall.

Figure 7: Yearly 13-week Overlay

Inflation and Money Supply

The chart below shows the history of inflation, Money Supply, and Fed Funds. As shown, in 1970 inflation worked with ~2 year lag compared to Money Supply. Given this, it is possible that another bout of inflation is lurking just under the surface considering the massive spikes in 2020 and 2021 and the Fed has already started cutting rates.

Figure 8: YoY M2 Change with CPI and Fed Funds

Historical Perspective

The charts below are designed to put the current trends into historical perspective. The orange bars represent annualized percentage change rather than raw dollar amount.

Figure 9: M2 with Growth Rate

Below shows the 13-week annualized average over history. This chart overlays the log return of the S&P. Mr. Wenzel proposed that large drops in Money Supply could be a sign of stock market pullbacks. His theory, derived from Murray Rothbard, states that when the market experiences a shrinking growth rate of Money Supply (or even negative) it can create liquidity issues in the stock market, leading to a sell off.

While not a perfect predictive tool, many of the dips in Money Supply precede market dips. Specifically, the major dips in 2002 and 2008 from +10% down to 0%. 2022 was highly correlated with a fall in Money Supply and the rebound has corresponded with the big stock market move we have seen recently.

Please note the chart only shows market data through Sept 30th to align with available M2 data.

Figure 10: 13-week M2 Annualized and S&P 500

One other consideration is the reverse repo market at the Fed. This is a tool that allows financial institutions to swap cash for instruments on the Fed balance sheet.

Reverse Repos peaked at $2.55T on Dec 30, 2022. Money gushed out from March 2023 to May 2024. The outflows have leveled off which may be one reason the stock market gains have been more muted since June.

Figure 11: Fed Reverse Repurchase Agreements

Wrapping Up

Money Supply can be a leading indicator and help explain the action in the stock market. Money Supply fell the entire year of 2022, bottomed in early 2023 was rising up until the recent period where it flattened out. Keeping an eye on Money Supply can help one understand how much wind the stock market has at its back.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Etsy: It Is All A Matter Of Operations
data.description:
Like most people, I also discovered Etsy during the COVID-19 pandemic. I bought a personalized kitchen apron for my mom; I enjoyed the platform, the graphic was neat and lovely, and the payment was smooth, but I remained troubled with the delivery time. It took almost two weeks to receive the package. Fortunately, I was looking for the gift a lot of time earlier than her birthday because, during the pandemic, there was a lot of free time. After receiving the apron, I deleted the app and never installed it again.

It was not because I did not like the overall experience, but simply because once things got back to normal, I do not have time anymore to plan for gifts three weeks in advance. I remember what I said to my mother in 2020: "If the company can improve its operations, particularly the shipping side, it could be a good investment".

Since then, the stock price has increased to almost $300 per share and the reasons are twofold:

  • The market rebound after the COVID collapse
  • The enhanced performance of the company is mainly due to the arrival on the platform of people like me who just made one or two purchases and then uninstalled the app.

In the article I will first discuss the business strategy of the company, I will then analyze its financials, study the risks, and finally, I will conclude with a valuation.

Strategy

For those who do not know it, Etsy connects people looking for unique and personalized goods with independent sellers around the world. That is the unique strength of the brand, unlike other e-commerce, on Etsy, you can find something that is often handcrafted, personalized, and original.

It is pretty straightforward to understand that the two most important assets of the company are the sellers and the buyers. Considering the company's 2023 financial report it has around 7 million sellers and 92 million active buyers.

Both figures have increased a lot with respect to 2019, mainly due to the pandemic effect that forced people to buy stuff online. In particular, in 2023, the number of sellers increased 29% with respect to 2022 and 176% with respect to 2019.

The situation is similar for the buyers who have increased by around 3% from 2022 and around 101% with respect to 2019.

Let's put it that way: the company did a very good job of retaining the highest number of buyers possible after the pandemic, but the number of new buyers is slowing. It looks like the company is not able to attract new customers, even though marketing expenses have increased by around 17% over the last couple of years.

More than marketing, I believe that keeping fees tight and reducing the delivery time is the best way for Etsy to attract new customers and enhance the GMS performance.

However, fees have always increased and have reached pretty high levels. You can find a lot of forums online or YouTube videos of people complaining about it. In particular, transaction fees are at 6.5% and every seller has a listing fee of $0.20 for every item listed for sale on the company website.

Competitors, on average, do have relatively lower fees. Facebook marketplace, for instance, has selling fees of 5% while on Vinted there are no fees at all for the sellers. This difference causes sellers to prefer these cheaper alternatives and inevitably buyers follow. After all, a marketplace is only as vibrant as the sellers who populate it. If sellers find better conditions elsewhere, the flow of both products and customers shifts with them, creating a domino effect.

The situation is not so different when taking into account the delivery time. The company estimates 3 to 7 business days for domestic deliveries in the U.S., while 10 to 30 business days for international shipments. I believe that in 2024, it is unacceptable to offer such a low standard for shipments, particularly in a very competitive industry such as e-commerce.

How can Etsy compete with Amazon Handmade, which, thanks to the Amazon fulfillment service, anything that you order arrives the following day?

Etsy used to have a competitive advantage some years ago, being the first e-commerce connecting artisans and small entrepreneurs with people looking for original and unique items. Now that other alternatives have arisen and offer either faster shipment or lower selling fees, I perceive that this competitive advantage has disappeared and the company will end up losing market shares in the future.

Financials

I prefer to observe the bigger picture rather than focus on quarterly results. At the end of the day, my investment strategy revolves around the long term rather than the short term.

Income Statement

To do things properly, I've decided to re-engineer the financial statements to have a clearer idea of the main accounting numbers.

Since 2021, revenues have grown by 18%. This could be seen as a good result for someone, but it is not that good actually if we consider the inflation rate in the U.S. was pretty high over the last years: 4.7% in 2021, 8% in 2022, and 4.1% in 2023. In other words, the amount of products sold was pretty much the same in 2023 as in 2021, what increased was the price of the products sold, and the commissions retained from the sellers.

Both the cost of revenue and the operating expenses increased more than the revenues and, therefore, ended up lowering profitability margins.

The situation is not so different when analyzing the 2024 first six months' results. Revenues increased only 1.9% with respect to 2023 while net income decreased around 15% from the previous year. Considering the management's forecasts and the general macroeconomic conditions, I do not see any major reverse of this trend anytime soon.

Balance Sheet

Etsy is a light capital business, unlike manufacturing or construction companies, it does not need a lot of assets to operate. This allows the company to have a lot of flexibility, reacting quickly to market changes or new opportunities that arise, but to be exposed to low barriers to entry that allow other companies to take part in the market segment without huge investments.

The real strength of the balance sheet is the negative net working capital. This means that the company has a lot of contractual strength with its suppliers and clients. This is perfectly in line with the company's business model: the money is immediately collected from clients when they purchase, while the company pays the suppliers (the sellers in this case) later, usually between 14 and 20 business days.

The long-term debt is quite high and is approaching the maximum limit I’m willing to allow for a company: its debt-to-equity ratio is slightly over 2. However, I have to say that the interest on debt is not so high, it does not contribute so much to lowering the company's profitability. To be precise, the company has three rounds of debt:

  • 0.125% convertible senior notes due 2026
  • 0.125% convertible senior notes due 2027
  • 0.25% convertible senior notes due 2028

I believe that even though the amount of debt seems high at first glance, it is fine considering that the interests the company has to pay on it are very low.

Cash Flow Statement

Being light capital allows the company to limit its investments in capital expenditures, moreover considering its ability to have a negative working capital allows the company to have a very good cash conversion cycle, therefore obtaining a positive free cash flow.

The company's free cash flow has grown at just below 3% CAGR over the last 4 years. The TTM free cash flow right now stands at $600 million, which represents a solid 7% growth from 2023. Considering the company's contractual strength, and its light capital business model that does not require major investments in CAPEX, its free cash flow can continue growing at a good pace also in future years.

This good result in the free cash flow, however, could be misleading for what concerns the company's valuation, as we will see in the valuation paragraph.

Risks

As usual, a complete list of risks can be found in the company's annual report.

In this paragraph, I would like to shed some light on the management team's ability to perform well in the future. I try to explain my idea a little bit better.

First of all, if you have a proper look at the 2023 financial report, on the opportunity side, you can find some very interesting hints about the prospects of the company. In this section, I would like to report three:

  • Capturing more of our total available market;
  • Continuing to expand beyond our top geographies;
  • Continuing to retain and reactivate lapsed buyers.

Each of these points is then explained in detail in a paragraph; the problem is that the company spends all the words talking about the outcomes of such strategies rather than how to implement them effectively. It seems like a fairy tale in which the managers try to sell to themselves first and to shareholders later.

This situation reminds me of the Tattooed Chef failure in 2022. The management team at the time was similarly trying to sell a narrative that ultimately turned out to be completely hollow. Here, the situation is different because the company brings solid financial results. However, the guide imposed by the current management team does not really inspire me, and I would prefer to see a clearer long-term plan before deciding to invest in the company.

I see Etsy as a company with great potential to expand and grow, but I do not see the current management team as good enough to seize the opportunity.

Moreover, the company is subject to a pretty high level of insider trading, with a lot of directors and officers selling their shares. Even the CEO has sold a lot of shares during 2024, for a total value of almost $5 million. Seeing company officers dump shares in a young company with strong growth potential is one of the things I dislike the most. It makes me immediately steer clear of its stock.

Valuation

Considering the positive and growing free cash flow that the company can generate, I believe that the best way to analyze the company is through a dcf.

First of all, I computed the WACC as demonstrated on the right-hand side of the sheet, and I obtained an 11.06% as a result.

I went ahead by estimating the future free cash flows of the company. Considering that over the last 5 years, the company had a free cash flow margin of around 20.1%, I projected that multiple on future revenue estimates, obtaining the free cash flows reported in the table below.

Considering the WACC as our discounting multiple, projecting a 2% perpetual growth rate, and considering the net financial position of the company, I obtained an equity value of $7.7B. The result is around 25% higher than the current price, therefore concluding that the company looks slightly undervalued to its intrinsic value.

However, I feel like a 25% discount is not enough for me to pull the trigger when considering that the company management team lacks a clear strategy for enhancing the business operations and continues to offload shares. Moreover, the company already has high debt, and revenue growth continues to stagnate. That is the reason why I assign a "hold" rating to the company, and I wait for further price drops or a clear sign of operational improvements.

Conclusion

Even though the stock price is compelling, I prefer to wait on the sidelines. At the end of the day, the only rule that you must follow as an investor is to not lose your money, and Etsy and its management team do not make me feel confident enough to pull the trigger.

I would prefer to see the company return to higher growth rates through a clear and efficient long-term strategy. I have to admit that, operationally, not much has changed since I was last interested in the company back in 2020. To conclude, I’d like to pose a question to the readers: Do you think the company would benefit from a new CEO?
